coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with excitation-independent emission for selective bioimaging and gene delivery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xudong
Wang, Yang
Shen, Xiran
Su, Chunyan
Yang, Jinghua
Piao, Mingjun
Jia, Fei
Gao, Guanghui
Zhang, Long
Lin, Quan
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots), as new members of the quantum sized carbon analogues have attracted significant attention due to their unique size, less toxicity, good compatibility and relatively easy surface modification. In this work, we report a simple, low-cost and one-step hydrothermal carbonization approach to synthesize the positively charged C-dots using PEI and FA. From the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, the as-prepared C-dots exhibit good stability and intense PL with the high quantum yield (QY) at Ca. 42%. Significantly, The as-prepared C-dots integrate the advantages of C-dots and PEI: the presence of C-dots can effectively decrease the cytotoxicity of PEI, the C-dots can be applied in biological system for selective imaging of folate receptor (FR)-positive cancerous cells from normal cells, while the cationic PEI with positive charges can make them link to plasmid DNA and efficiently transfect the therapeutic plasmid into cells. Therefore, the as-prepared with the facile synthesis method can be a promising photoluminescent probe for cancer diagnosis and gene therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Selective image
Cancer diagnosis
Gene therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell transfection and transfection efficiency determination
Synthesis and characterization of the C-dots
Cancer cells detection using C-dots as the fluorescent probes
Gene delivery using C-dots as the gene vector

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustrates the synthesis of photoluminescent C-dots from FA and PEI via hydrothermal carbonization approach.
(a) TEM image of synthesized C-dots, scale bar: 20nm. Inset: HRTEM image of C-dots, scale bar: 2nm; (b) the corresponding particle size distribution histograms.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) XPS and (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of as-prepared C-dots.

(a) UV–vis absorption (1), PL excitation (2) and emission (3) spectra of C-dots in aqueous solution (λex =370nm). Inserts show photographs of the C-dots in aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light. (b) PL emission spectra of C-dots in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths (from 310 to 410nm in 10nm increments).
Viability of 293T cells (a) and HeLa cells (b) after 24, 48 and 72h of incubation with different concentrations of C-dots in the cell medium as determined by MTT assays.
Confocal images of 293T cells (a) and HeLa cells (b) incubated with C-dots for 4h; (i) bright-field, (ii) confocal fluorescent and (iii) overlay images.

(a) Fluorescent images of EGFP expression in 293T cells and (b) in HeLa cells obtained by ultraviolet fluorescence microscope. (c) Flow cytometry assay of transfection in 293T cells and (d) HeLa cells.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with excitation-independent emission for selective bioimaging and gene delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, low-cost and one-step hydrothermal treatment for the synthesis of positively charged carbon dots from FA and PEI. They exhibit excellent photoluminescence property and can be promising nanomaterials for cancer diagnosis and gene therapy.

Photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots), as new members of the quantum sized carbon analogues have attracted significant attention due to their unique size, less toxicity, good compatibility and relatively easy surface modification. In this work, we report a simple, low-cost and one-step hydrothermal carbonization approach to synthesize the positively charged C-dots using PEI and FA. From the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, the as-prepared C-dots exhibit good stability and intense PL with the high quantum yield (QY) at Ca. 42%. Significantly, The as-prepared C-dots integrate the advantages of C-dots and PEI: the presence of C-dots can effectively decrease the cytotoxicity of PEI, the C-dots can be applied in biological system for selective imaging of folate receptor (FR)-positive cancerous cells from normal cells, while the cationic PEI with positive charges can make them link to plasmid DNA and efficiently transfect the therapeutic plasmid into cells. Therefore, the as-prepared with the facile synthesis method can be a promising photoluminescent probe for cancer diagnosis and gene therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last three decades, carbon-based materials have attracted significant attention due to their unique size, less toxicity, good compatibility and relatively esay surface modification, etc. [1–6]. Carbon dots (C-dots), as new members of the quantum sized carbon analogues have been applied widely in the fields of bioimaging [6–8], printing inks [2,6], sensors [9–11], photocatalysis [12], electronic and optoelectronic devices [13–16]. To date, various synthesis methods have been developed for the synthesis of C-dots, such as fragmentation/fractionation of suitable carbon source followed by surface passivation or alternatively by bottom-up strategies utilizing molecular precursors, and some other techniques such as combustion, thermal, or microwave treatments [2,3,17–24]. Among these reports, one of most studied properties is the excitation-dependent vs the excitation-independent features of emission for C-dots [24,25]. It has been reported that the C-dots exhibit the excitation-dependent PL behaviors, which usually reflects the effect of particle size distribution and surface states [26,27]. While the C-dots exhibiting the excitation-independent PL behavior suggests surface states rather than morphology, and surface states should be rather uniform [24].

Bio-imaging and gene therapy provide promising paradigms for the diagnosis and treatment of various diseases, including cancers, cystic fibrosis, hepatitis and sickle-cell anemia [28,29–32]. Despite the promise of bio-imaging and gene therapy, there still exist many challenging subjects. The main challenge is to find the suitable materials for imaging or delivery the genetic materials (DNA, RNA, small interfering RNAs, microRNAs) to within the target cells [33–37]. To achieve this aim of gene therapy, much effort has been devoted to developing the gene carriers, which can be broadly categorized into two groups: viral and nonviral [28,38,39]. Although viruses have shown promise as effective transfection vectors; issues of immunogenicity, carcinogenicity, and in ammation raise serious concerns for clinical applications. The main features of these materials for clinical application are low toxicity, cell targeted and high transfection efficiency [39–41]. To date, much effort has been devoted to developing the bio-imaging agents and gene carriers. In particular, targeted fluorescent materials for cancer cell imaging and gene delivery are highly desirable for cancer diagnosis and treatment. Folate receptor (FR) is well known to be upregulated in a variety of human cancerous cells, including the malignancies of ovary, mammary gland, lung, kidney, brain, prostate and throat; while in other cells FR is only minimally distributed [42–45]. For FR, folic acid (FA) conjugated with fluorescent nanoparticles and molecules have been widely used for cellular imaging, and such technology has also been employed as an important strategies to distinguish the cancer cell from normal cells [43,45–49]. As cationic polymers, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and poly-l-lysine have already applied for gene delivery owing to their synthetic maneuverability and high DNA-binding ability [28,38,50–54].

In this work, a facile, low-cost and one-step hydrothermal carbonization route for the preparation of water-soluble photoluminescent C-dots with FA and PEI as carbon source and passivation reagent respectively was presented. The C-dots exhibit positively charged and excitation-independent emission. The as-prepared C-dots integrate the advantages of C-dots and PEI: the presence of C-dots can effectively decrease the cytotoxicity of PEI, the C-dots can be applied in biological system for selective imaging of folate receptor (FR)-positive cancerous cells from normal cells, while the cationic PEI with positive charges can make them link to plasmid DNA and efficiently transfect the therapeutic plasmid into cells. Therefore, the as-prepared C-dots with the facile synthesis method are potential to be used in biological and chemical fields, including biological imaging, cancer diagnosis, and externally gene therapy.

Folic acid (FA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight:1800) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The ultra-pure water used throughout the experiment was purified with a Milli-Q A10 filtration system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The morphology and mean diameter of C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) and High-resolution TEM (HTEM, FEI TECNAI F20). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a Lambad 800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet Avatrr 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Zetasizer-nanozs. Fluorescence spectra were performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer. XRD data were collected on a Siemens D–5005 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (λ=1.5418Å). The confocal microscopy images were taken with a Olympus Fluoview FV1000. All measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions

In a typical procedure, the positively charged C-dots were prepared as follows: Firstly, 0.5g FA and 2g PEI were dissolved into 10mL distilled water to form a well-distributed solution. Then the mixture was sealed into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, which was then placed in a muffle furnace followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 6h. During this process, the color of the solution changed from yellow to brown, which indicated the formation of C-dots. Then the C-dots solution was dissolved in DI water and centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min to remove small volumes of liquid and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. To further purify the C-dots, they were added to water and subjected to dialysis against pure water through a membrane (MWCO=3.5–5 KD, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 3h. Finally, the C-dots of clear, light, yellow–brown aqueous were obtained. After freeze drying processing, we obtained 1.03g of solid C-dots, the yield of C-dots is about 41.2%.

The QY of C-dots was calculated by comparing their integrated PL intensities (excitation at 365nm) and absorbance values at 365nm with those of quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate (QY=0.54) was dissolved in 0.1M of H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance value in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength. Excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5.0nm when recording their PL spectra. The QY was calculated by the following equation:
∅
x
=
∅
st
(
I
x
/
I
st
) (
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
) (
A
st
/
A
x
)
where ø is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “st” refers to standard with know QY and “x” for the sample.

The 293T and HeLa cells lines were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) MEDIA supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C and 5% CO2. 1×104 cells were seeded into a 96-well cell culture plate in cell culture medium. On the treatment day, the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of the medium containing the C-dots of different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100mg/L). The cells were then incubated for another 24, 48 and 72h, respectively. Then, 20μL of 5mg/L MTT solution was added to every cell well. The cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, and then 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for 10min at room temperature. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 490nm. Blank wells were that there were no cells, only treated with cell culture medium. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation:
Cell
Viability
( % ) = (
OD
Treated
/
OD
Control
) × 100 %
where ODControl is obtained in the absence of C-dos, and ODTreated is obtained in the presence of C-dots.

The 293T and HeLa cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C and 5% CO2, respectively. Suspensions (20μg/mL) of C-dots from the stock solution were prepared with serum-containing DMEM. 10×104 cells were seeded in a 6-well cell culture plate in cell cuture medium. On the treatment day, the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of the medium containing 20μL/mL C-dots. The cells were then incubated at 37°C for 4h. The excess C-dots were removed by washing 3 times with warm phosphate buffered saline (PBS).

The C-dots/pEGFP complex solution was prepared by premixing C-dots solution with pEGFP (0.4μg) at different weight ratios ranging from 0.5:1 to 10:1, and diluted by deionized water to the total volume of 9μL. Next, 1μL of loading buffer (10×) was added into each sample, and then the mixtures were checked by electrophoresis using 1.0% agarose gel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell transfection and transfection efficiency determination
ce:section.ce:para: The human cervical carcinoma cell line (Hela cells) and 293T cell line were obtained from Chinese Academy of Sciences Cell Bank (Shanghai, P. R. China). Cells were grown in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with high glucose (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, California) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U/mL penicillin G and 100μg/mL streptomycin at 37°C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. To transfect the cells in vitro, the cells were plated at 3×105 cells per well in a six-well plate with 2mL culture medium for 24h and replaced with fresh medium without FBS 1h before transfection. Before transfection, the culture medium was replaced with 1mL of serum-free DMEM, The positively charged C-dots previously mixed with enhanced green fluorescent protein DNA plasmid (pEGFP) complex at different w/w ratios of nanocomposites and plasmid DNA (pDNA). The amount of plasmid was constant at 2μg. After incubation for 4h, the transfection medium was removed, and complete culture medium was then added. Cells were incubated at 37°C to allow expression of the transfected gene. Forty-eight hours after transfection, the cells were observed by Olympus inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Melville, New York), then cells were washed twice with PBS and resuspended in 500μL of PBS. The transfection efficiency of the suspended cell samples was then analyzed by flow cytometry (BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental procedure of this work is depicted in Fig. 1 . FA and PEI were added to distilled water to form a well-distributed solution. The solution was then heated by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 6h. During this process, the color of the solution changed from yellow to brown, which indicated the formation of C-dots. The carbogenic nanoparticles were passivated by PEI, yielding photoluminescent C-dots. The corresponding reaction was conducted by first condensing FA and PEI under the hydrothermal treatment to form polymer-like C-dots, which were then carbonized to form the C-dots [6].

The size and morphology of C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) technique. As shown in Fig.2 a, The TEM images show that the C-dots are uniformly dispersed and spherical in shape with an average diameter of 5.8nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of C-dots (Fig.2a inset) shows the well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interlayer spacing of 0.221nm, which is very close to both the (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon and the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [12]. According to TEM measurement, the statistical particle size distribution of C-dots is mainly in the range of 2–9nm in diameter (Fig.2b).

As shown in Fig.3 a, a typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern displays a board peak located at 21.5°, similar to the graphite lattice spacing, which is attributed to the amorphous carbon phase. The surface functional groups of as-prepared C-dots were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig.3b, the broad absorption band at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned to v(OH) and v(NH). Absorption peaks at 2947cm−1, 1506cm−1, 1407cm−1, 1283cm−1 and 1186cm−1 are correspond to v(CH), δ(CH), δ(CNHC), δ(COC) and δ(COH), respectively. The peaks at 1630cm−1 and the signal at 1543cm−1 result from v(CO) and δ(N-H), respectively, which verified the presence of the NHCO group in the C-dots and demonstrated the forming process of the C-dots. Contrast with free FA molecule, the C-dots mainly retained the molecular structures of FA (Fig. S1). The surface groups are also investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The XPS measurement shows the existence of three elements, C, O and N with their contents 49.07%, 15.87% and 35.06%, respectively (Fig.3c). As shown in Fig.3d, the high resolution C1s spectrum displays four peaks at 284.5, 285.8, 286.6, and 287.8eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CN/CN and CO(O) groups, individually. Moreover, 13C NMR spectroscopy is a powerful technique in distinguishing sp3 hybridized carbon atoms from the sp2 ones. The existence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in as-prepared C-dots are confirmed from the 13C NMR spectrum of the C-dots in D2O (Fig. S2). In the 13C NMR spectrum, signals in the range of 30–60ppm, which correspond to aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms, and signals from 100–185ppm, which are indicative of sp2 carbon atoms, were observed. The signals in the range of 170–185ppm indicate the existence of carboxyl/amide groups. These results indicate that the C-dots are rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups on their surfaces. Moreover, These function groups improve their hydrophilicity and stability in the aqueous system.

To further explore the optical properties of the C-dots, UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra were researched at room temperature. As shown in Fig.4 a, the UV–vis spectrum of the aqueous solution exhibits a sharp peak and a broad absorption band with peaks at 272nm and 356nm respectively. The peak at 272nm is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, leads to nearly no observed PL signal. The other transition centered about 356nm due to the trapping of excited stats results in strong emission. In the PL spectra, the C-dots in aqueous solution have excitation and emission wavelengths at 370nm and 452nm, respectively. The solution exhibits light yellow color under ambient light, while bright blue emission was observed under irradiation with a 365nm UV lamp. As depicted in Fig.4b, with excitation from 310 to 410nm, the corresponding PL emission peaks of the C-dots do not show great shifts and exhibit the excitation-independent emission behavior. This behavior is the result of the surface state/molecular state and particle size distribution of the C-dots. Furthermore, the strongest PL emission band, located at 452nm, is obtained under 370nm excitation and shows a high quantum yield of 42% (using quinine sulfate as a reference, Table S1). For nitrogen doped C-dots, it has been reported that the surface detect passivated by nitrogen would act as excitation energy traps and impact on the PL properties of C-dots. The higher content of nitrogen would increase the quantum yield of C-dots [24]. The decay curve at 452nm can be fitted by a double-exponential function with lifetimes of 8.19ns (87.80%) and 4.00ns (12.20%) at 370nm excitation, with an intensity-weighted average time of 7.678ns (Fig. S3). Moreover, a high photostability was observed from the C-dots, as illustrated in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer cells detection using C-dots as the fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, low toxicity is an essential factor for PL materials applied in biological application. The MTT (3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetra-zoliu- -mromide) assays and apoptosis assay were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of C-dots and the viability of cells. 293T and HeLa cells were cultured in the medium containing different concentration of C-dots for 24, 48 and 72h respectively. From the results of the MTT assay (Fig. 5 ), the viability of 293T and HeLa cells remained above 85% after incubation with C-dots even at the concentration of 100mg/L for 72h. As a result, the as-prepared C-dots are suitable nanomaterials for imaging and gene delivery in cells.

The application of C-dots to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells was evaluated by culturing and analyzing model cells of 293T and HeLa cells. The 293T and HeLa cells are selected as normal and cancerous cells, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , there is no fluorescence signal in the 293T cells incubated with C-dots for 4h, while the HeLa cells incubated with C-dots for 4h exhibit bright fluorescence. Compared with normal 293T cells, the HeLa cells show sensitive endocytosis of the C-dots in a short time. These results are probably due to the specific interactions between FA of C-dots and folate receptors in the HeLa cancerous cell membrance, which should favor folate receptor mediated endocytosis. These performances imply that our C-dots can simply discrimination of FR-positive cancerous cells from normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gene delivery using C-dots as the gene vector
ce:section.ce:para: The zeta potential value of as-prepared C-dots is +23.5mV at pH 7.0 due to the positive charge of protonated amino groups of PEI (Fig. S5). The positively charged C-dots can bond with the negative charges of plasmid DNA (pDNA). The zeta potential value of C-dots/pDNA changes to +10.6mV due to the neutralization of pDNA (Fig. S6). These positive charges also allow the C-dots/pDNA composites to attach to weak negative charges of cell membrance. The agarose gel electrophoresis assay was used to investigate the ability of the C-dots to adsorb pDNA at different complex ratios. As displayed in Fig. S7, no free pDNA band could be detected with the weight ratio of C-dots/pDNA was above 8.0, suggesting that the C-dots surface could efficiently capture pDNA owing to the electrostatic interactions between positively charged amine groups in PEI and negatively charged DNA molecules. To visualize the gene expression in vitro, transfection experiment between the C-dots and pDNA in 293T and HeLa cells respectively, and EGFP complementary DNA in plasmid (pEGFP) as a reporter gene. As shown in Fig.7 a and b, the 293T and HeLa cells transfected by C-dots/pEGFP nanoparticles respectively. As can be seen, strong green fluorescence can be observed from the 293T and HeLa cells, which indicated the successful delivery and expression of EGFP. To further quantify the transfection efficiency, flow cytometry was used. Fig.7c and d shows the percentages of positive EGFP cells is 24.53% in the 293T cells, 42.63% in the HeLa cells. These results indicate that the positively charged C-dots can efficiently transfect the cells. The as-prepared positively charged C-dots show potential for application in gene delivery and gene therapy.

In summary, we report a facile method to prepare the water-soluble photoluminescent C-dots under the hydrothermal condition. The method is relatively simple, economic, and environmentally friendly. The as-obtained C-dots show high quantum yield, excellent ability and good biocompatibility. We adopt FA and PEI to prepare the C-dots with positive charges. Moreover, the C-dots can be directly applied in the imaging of cells without further functionalization and show specific targeting ability to cancer cells that overexpress the FA receptor. The positively charged C-dots also can link to plasmid DNA and efficiently transfect the therapeutic plasmid into cells. Therefore, the C-dots with the facile synthesis method can be a promising material for cancer diagnosis and gene therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51373061, 51603018 and 51473023), the Project for Science & Technology Development of Jilin Province (20150520012JH and 20130206067SF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.12.057.
